title: IBM to Commission One of India's First Physical Quantum Computers in Amaravati by September 2026 - Quantum Computing Report
gist: IBM CEO Arvind Krishna confirmed that Amaravati will host one of India's first two on-shore physical IBM quantum computers, an IBM Quantum System Two with a 156-qubit Heron processor, targeted for commissioning by September 2026 under a trilateral MoU with Andhra Pradesh and TCS. Krishna framed commercial quantum advantage as two to three years away, making early physical-infrastructure ownership critical for local IP and sovereign capability.

insights.claim: IBM CEO Arvind Krishna confirmed the greenfield city of Amaravati will host one of the first two physical IBM quantum computers deployed on-shore in India, targeted for full operational commissioning by September 2026 as an anchor for Andhra Pradesh's Quantum Valley initiative.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A dated, named on-shore deployment is a concrete milestone for India's sovereign quantum ambitions and IBM's hardware export footprint; watch September 2026 for slippage.
insights.locator: IBM Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Arvind Krishna

insights.claim: The Amaravati site is planned to house an IBM Quantum System Two configured with a 156-qubit Heron quantum processor, subject to standard international export licensing frameworks and definitive technical agreements.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Export-licensing dependency is the key execution risk; a 156-qubit Heron sets the concrete capability baseline India is acquiring.
insights.locator: The upcoming hardware pipeline follows a trilateral

insights.claim: The deployment follows a trilateral memorandum of understanding executed between the Government of Andhra Pradesh, IBM, and Tata Consultancy Services.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: TCS's involvement points to a systems-integration and services revenue channel forming around India's quantum buildout.
insights.locator: The upcoming hardware pipeline follows a trilateral

insights.claim: IBM CEO Arvind Krishna stated that commercial realization of quantum computing across pharmaceuticals, advanced AI, financial fraud analysis, and materials science is roughly two to three years away from delivering market advantages, making early infrastructure ownership critical for developing localized intellectual property.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A two-to-three-year commercial-advantage window from IBM's CEO is a datable investment-clock anchor; use it to time exposure to quantum end-markets.
insights.locator: According to Krishna, the commercial realization

insights.claim: The Amaravati Quantum Reference Facility is backed by India's roughly 6,000-crore-rupee National Quantum Mission and convenes more than 50 foundational partners, including Larsen & Toubro, C-DAC, and C-DOT, plus academic institutions to build an end-to-end local supply chain.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: A 50+ partner ecosystem under national funding maps the Indian quantum supply-chain beneficiaries; L&T, C-DAC, and C-DOT are the named on-shore participants to track.
insights.locator: The structural development at the Quantum Valley Tech Park

insights.claim: The deployment aims to shift India's role from a remote consumer of cloud-routed compute blocks to a localized sovereign developer of physical quantum hardware, cultivating regional talent capable of designing unhackable national-security communications and running molecular drug-discovery simulations locally.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: The consumer-to-sovereign-developer framing is the strategic rationale for the spend; national-security use cases signal durable state demand insulated from commercial cycles.
insights.locator: The on-premises installation aims to position
